lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: Is she to be buried in Christian burial that wilfully seeks her own salvation?

lines.speaker: SECOND CLOWN
lines.text: I tell thee she is: and therefore make her grave straight: the

lines.speaker: SECOND CLOWN
lines.text: crowner hath sat on her, and finds it Christian burial.

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: How can that be, unless she drowned herself in her own defence?

lines.speaker: SECOND CLOWN
lines.text: Why, ’tis found so.

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: It must be “se offendendo;” it cannot be else. For here lies

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: the point: if I drown myself wittingly, it argues an act: and

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: an act hath three branches; it is, to act, to do, to

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: perform: argal, she drowned herself wittingly.

lines.speaker: SECOND CLOWN
lines.text: Nay, but hear you, goodman delver—

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: Give me leave. Here lies the water; good: here stands the man;

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: good: if the man go to this water, and drown himself, it

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: is, will he, nill he, he goes—mark you that; but if the

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: water come to him and drown him, he drowns not himself: argal,

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: he that is not guilty of his own death shortens not his

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: own life.

lines.speaker: SECOND CLOWN
lines.text: But is this law?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: Ay, marry, is’t; crowner’s quest law.

lines.speaker: SECOND CLOWN
lines.text: Will you ha’ the truth on’t? If this had not been a

lines.speaker: SECOND CLOWN
lines.text: gentlewoman, she should have been buried out o’ Christian burial.

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: Why, there thou say’st: and the more pity that great folk should

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: have countenance in this world to drown or hang themselves, more than

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: their even Christian. Come, my spade. There is no ancient gentleman but

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: gardeners, ditchers, and grave-makers: they hold up Adam’s profession.

lines.speaker: SECOND CLOWN
lines.text: Was he a gentleman?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: A’ was the first that ever bore arms.

lines.speaker: SECOND CLOWN
lines.text: Why, he had none.

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: What, art a heathen? How dost thou understand the Scripture? The Scripture

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: says “Adam digged:” could he dig without arms? I’ll put another question

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: to thee: if thou answerest me not to the purpose, confess thyself—

lines.speaker: SECOND CLOWN
lines.text: Go to.

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: What is he that builds stronger than either the mason, the shipwright, or the carpenter?

lines.speaker: SECOND CLOWN
lines.text: The gallows-maker; for that frame outlives a thousand tenants.

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: I like thy wit well, in good faith: the gallows does well;

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: but how does it well? it does well to those that do

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: ill: now thou dost ill to say the gallows is built stronger

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: than the church: argal, the gallows may do well to thee. To’t

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: again, come.

lines.speaker: SECOND CLOWN
lines.text: “Who builds stronger than a mason, a shipwright, or a carpenter?”

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: Ay, tell me that, and unyoke.

lines.speaker: SECOND CLOWN
lines.text: Marry, now I can tell.

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: To’t.

lines.speaker: SECOND CLOWN
lines.text: Mass, I cannot tell.

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: Cudgel thy brains no more about it, for your dull ass will

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: not mend his pace with beating; and, when you are asked this

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: question next, say “a grave-maker:” the houses that he makes last till

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: doomsday. Go, get thee to Yaughan: fetch me a stoup of liquor.

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: He digs and sings. In youth, when I did love, did love,

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: Methought it was very sweet, To contract, O, the time, for, ah,

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: my behove, O, methought, there was nothing meet.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Has this fellow no feeling of his business, that he sings at grave-making?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Custom hath made it in him a property of easiness.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: ’Tis e’en so: the hand of little employment hath the daintier sense.

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: But age, with his stealing steps, Hath claw’d me in his clutch,

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: And hath shipped me intil the land, As if I had never

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: been such.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That skull had a tongue in it, and could sing once: how

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: the knave jowls it to the ground, as if it were Cain’s

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: jaw-bone, that did the first murder! It might be the pate of

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: a politician, which this ass now o’er-reaches; one that would circumvent God,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: might it not?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: It might, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Or of a courtier; which could say “Good morrow, sweet lord! How

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: dost thou, good lord?” This might be my lord such-a-one, that praised

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: my lord such-a-one’s horse, when he meant to beg it; might it

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: not?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Ay, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why, e’en so: and now my Lady Worm’s; chapless, and knocked about

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: the mazzard with a sexton’s spade: here’s fine revolution, an we had

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: the trick to see’t. Did these bones cost no more the breeding,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: but to play at loggats with ’em? mine ache to think on’t.

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: A pick-axe, and a spade, a spade, For and a shrouding sheet:

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: O, a pit of clay for to be made For such a

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: guest is meet.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: There’s another: why may not that be the skull of a lawyer?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Where be his quiddities now, his quillets, his cases, his tenures, and

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: his tricks? why does he suffer this rude knave now to knock

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: him about the sconce with a dirty shovel, and will not tell

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: him of his action of battery? Hum! This fellow might be in’s

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: time a great buyer of land, with his statutes, his recognizances, his

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: fines, his double vouchers, his recoveries: is this the fine of his

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: fines, and the recovery of his recoveries, to have his fine pate

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: full of fine dirt? will his vouchers vouch him no more of

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: his purchases, and double ones too, than the length and breadth of

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: a pair of indentures? The very conveyances of his lands will hardly

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: lie in this box; and must the inheritor himself have no more,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: ha?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Not a jot more, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Is not parchment made of sheepskins?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Ay, my lord, and of calf-skins too.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: They are sheep and calves which seek out assurance in that. I

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: will speak to this fellow. Whose grave’s this, sirrah?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: Mine, sir. O, a pit of clay for to be made For

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: such a guest is meet.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I think it be thine, indeed; for thou liest in’t.

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: You lie out on’t, sir, and therefore it is not yours: for

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: my part, I do not lie in’t, and yet it is mine.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Thou dost lie in’t, to be in’t and say it is thine:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: ’tis for the dead, not for the quick; therefore thou liest.

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: ’Tis a quick lie, sir; ’twill away again, from me to you.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: What man dost thou dig it for?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: For no man, sir.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: What woman, then?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: For none, neither.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Who is to be buried in’t?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: One that was a woman, sir; but, rest her soul, she’s dead.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: How absolute the knave is! we must speak by the card, or

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: equivocation will undo us. By the Lord, Horatio, these three years I

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: have taken a note of it; the age is grown so picked

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: that the toe of the peasant comes so near the heel of

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: the courtier, he galls his kibe. How long hast thou been a

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: grave-maker?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: Of all the days i’ the year, I came to’t that day

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: that our last king Hamlet overcame Fortinbras.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: How long is that since?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: Cannot you tell that? every fool can tell that: it was the

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: very day that young Hamlet was born; he that is mad, and

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: sent into England.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Ay, marry, why was he sent into England?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: Why, because he was mad: he shall recover his wits there; or,

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: if he do not, it’s no great matter there.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: ’Twill not be seen in him there; there the men are as mad as he.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: How came he mad?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: Very strangely, they say.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: How strangely?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: Faith, e’en with losing his wits.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Upon what ground?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: Why, here in Denmark: I have been sexton here, man and boy, thirty years.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: How long will a man lie i’ the earth ere he rot?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: I’ faith, if he be not rotten before he die—as we have

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: many pocky corses now-a-days, that will scarce hold the laying in—he will

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: last you some eight year or nine year: a tanner will last

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: you nine year.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why he more than another?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: Why, sir, his hide is so tanned with his trade, that he

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: will keep out water a great while; and your water is a

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: sore decayer of your whoreson dead body. Here’s a skull now; this

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: skull has lain in the earth three and twenty years.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Whose was it?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: A whoreson mad fellow’s it was: whose do you think it was?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Nay, I know not.

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: A pestilence on him for a mad rogue! a’ poured a flagon

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: of Rhenish on my head once. This same skull, sir, was Yorick’s

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: skull, the king’s jester.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: This?

lines.speaker: FIRST CLOWN
lines.text: E’en that.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Let me see. Alas, poor Yorick! I knew him, Horatio: a fellow

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: of infinite jest, of most excellent fancy: he hath borne me on

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: his back a thousand times; and now, how abhorred in my imagination

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: it is! my gorge rises at it. Here hung those lips that

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I have kissed I know not how oft. Where be your gibes

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: now? your gambols? your songs? your flashes of merriment, that were wont

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: to set the table on a roar? Not one now, to mock

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: your own grinning? quite chap-fallen? Now get you to my lady’s chamber,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: and tell her, let her paint an inch thick, to this favour

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: she must come; make her laugh at that. Prithee, Horatio, tell me

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: one thing.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: What’s that, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Dost thou think Alexander looked o’ this fashion i’ the earth?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: E’en so.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And smelt so? pah!

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: E’en so, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To what base uses we may return, Horatio! Why may not imagination

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: trace the noble dust of Alexander, till he find it stopping a

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: bung-hole?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: ’Twere to consider too curiously, to consider so.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: No, faith, not a jot; but to follow him thither with modesty

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: enough, and likelihood to lead it: as thus: Alexander died, Alexander was

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: buried, Alexander returneth into dust; the dust is earth; of earth we

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: make loam; and why of that loam, whereto he was converted, might

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: they not stop a beer-barrel? Imperious Caesar, dead and turn’d to clay,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Might stop a hole to keep the wind away: O, that that

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: earth, which kept the world in awe, Should patch a wall to

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: expel the winter flaw! But soft! but soft! aside: here comes the

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: king,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: What ceremony else?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That is Laertes,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: A very noble youth: mark.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: What ceremony else?

lines.speaker: FIRST PRIEST
lines.text: Her obsequies have been as far enlarged

lines.speaker: FIRST PRIEST
lines.text: As we have warranty: her death was doubtful;

lines.speaker: FIRST PRIEST
lines.text: And, but that great command o’ersways the order,

lines.speaker: FIRST PRIEST
lines.text: She should in ground unsanctified have lodged

lines.speaker: FIRST PRIEST
lines.text: Till the last trumpet; for charitable prayers,

lines.speaker: FIRST PRIEST
lines.text: Shards, flints and pebbles should be thrown on her:

lines.speaker: FIRST PRIEST
lines.text: Yet here she is allow’d her virgin crants,

lines.speaker: FIRST PRIEST
lines.text: Her maiden strewments and the bringing home

lines.speaker: FIRST PRIEST
lines.text: Of bell and burial.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Must there no more be done?

lines.speaker: FIRST PRIEST
lines.text: No more be done:

lines.speaker: FIRST PRIEST
lines.text: We should profane the service of the dead

lines.speaker: FIRST PRIEST
lines.text: To sing a requiem and such rest to her

lines.speaker: FIRST PRIEST
lines.text: As to peace-parted souls.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Lay her i’ the earth:

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: And from her fair and unpolluted flesh

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: May violets spring! I tell thee, churlish priest,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: A ministering angel shall my sister be,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: When thou liest howling.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: What, the fair Ophelia!

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Sweets to the sweet: farewell! Scattering flowers.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: I hoped thou shouldst have been my Hamlet’s wife;

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: I thought thy bride-bed to have deck’d, sweet maid,

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: And not have strew’d thy grave.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: O, treble woe

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Fall ten times treble on that cursed head,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Whose wicked deed thy most ingenious sense

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Deprived thee of! Hold off the earth awhile,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Till I have caught her once more in mine arms: Leaps into the grave.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Now pile your dust upon the quick and dead,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Till of this flat a mountain you have made,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: To o’ertop old Pelion, or the skyish head

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Of blue Olympus.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Advancing. What is he whose grief

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Bears such an emphasis? whose phrase of sorrow

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Conjures the wandering stars, and makes them stand

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Like wonder-wounded hearers? This is I,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Hamlet the Dane. Leaps into the grave.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: The devil take thy soul! Grappling with him.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Thou pray’st not well.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I prithee, take thy fingers from my throat;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: For, though I am not splenitive and rash,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Yet have I something in me dangerous,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Which let thy wiseness fear: hold off thy hand.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Pluck them asunder.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Hamlet, Hamlet!

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Gentlemen—

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Good my lord, be quiet. The Attendants part them, and they come

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: out of the grave.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why, I will fight with him upon this theme

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Until my eyelids will no longer wag.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: O my son, what theme?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I loved Ophelia: forty thousand brothers

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Could not, with all their quantity of love,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Make up my sum. What wilt thou do for her?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: O, he is mad, Laertes.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: For love of God, forbear him.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: ’Swounds, show me what thou’lt do:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Woo’t weep? woo’t fight? woo’t fast? woo’t tear thyself?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Woo’t drink up eisel? eat a crocodile?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I’ll do’t. Dost thou come here to whine?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To outface me with leaping in her grave?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Be buried quick with her, and so will I:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And, if thou prate of mountains, let them throw

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Millions of acres on us, till our ground,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Singeing his pate against the burning zone,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Make Ossa like a wart! Nay, an thou’lt mouth,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I’ll rant as well as thou.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: This is mere madness:

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: And thus awhile the fit will work on him;

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Anon, as patient as the female dove,

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: When that her golden couplets are disclosed,

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: His silence will sit drooping.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Hear you, sir;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: What is the reason that you use me thus?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I loved you ever: but it is no matter;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Let Hercules himself do what he may,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The cat will mew and dog will have his day.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: I pray you, good Horatio, wait upon him.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To Laertes. Strengthen your patience in our last night’s speech;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: We’ll put the matter to the present push.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Good Gertrude, set some watch over your son.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: This grave shall have a living monument:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: An hour of quiet shortly shall we see;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Till then, in patience our proceeding be.

stage_directions.text: Enter two Clowns, with spades, etc.
Enter Hamlet and Horatio, at a distance.
Enter Priests, etc. in procession; the Corpse of Ophelia, Laertes and Mourners following; King, Queen, their trains, etc.

The queen, the courtiers: who is this they follow? And with such maimed rites? This doth betoken The corse they follow did with desperate hand Fordo its own life: ’twas of some estate. Couch we awhile, and mark. Retiring with Horatio.

glosses.definition: willfully seeks her own heaven by suicide (ironic)
carried weapons; also, a coat of arms
makes a more lasting frame than

annotations.title: The gravediggers and the theology of suicide
annotations.body: The clowns' opening argument about whether Ophelia deserves Christian burial parodies a real 1560 legal case, Hales v. Pettit, which asked whether a suicide could legally transfer property. The First Clown's 'Argal' (a corruption of 'ergo') mocks the legalistic reasoning of the original case. Shakespeare's audience would have laughed at the bad Latin and recognized the grim topic underneath: the Church of England denied Christian burial rites to self-murderers, and exceptions required 'great command o'ersways the order.' The scene establishes, before Hamlet arrives, that Ophelia's death sits under a theological and legal ambiguity — the play will never quite say whether her drowning was suicide, accident, or something between the two.
sources: Susan Snyder, 'The Genres of Shakespeare's Plays' (1979)

annotations.title: Hamlet in the graveyard: the return of the body
annotations.body: The prince who spent Act 3 in a state of abstract meditation on death now handles actual skulls. The change is registered in the verse: 'That skull had a tongue in it, and could sing once' is plain English, no longer dressed in the rhetoric of 'this mortal coil.' Hamlet has been in a boat with pirates, seen his own execution order, and confronted death as a specific thing rather than a philosophical topic. The graveyard scene tracks this development — skulls become individuals (a lawyer, a politician, a courtier, a jester) rather than types. The meditation is the inverse of Ecclesiastes: not 'all is vanity' but 'someone once kissed these bones.'

annotations.title: 'Alas, poor Yorick!'
annotations.body: Hamlet's encounter with Yorick's skull is the play's most anthologized moment, and the one most liable to sentimental misreading. The speech is not melancholy but astonished: the jester who carried young Hamlet on his back now fits in the prince's hand. 'Where be your gibes now? your gambols? your songs? your flashes of merriment, that were wont to set the table on a roar?' The specific memory makes the grief personal in a way the abstract soliloquies could not. What follows ('Alexander died, Alexander was buried, Alexander returneth into dust') pushes the lesson outward again — but the Yorick moment itself is irreducible. A remembered child's laughter survives in an empty skull, and that survival is stranger than any philosophy can manage.

annotations.title: The grave-leap and the staging of rivalry
annotations.body: Laertes leaps into Ophelia's grave; Hamlet leaps in after him. The physical confrontation inside the grave is one of Shakespeare's most daring stage images: two mourners fighting for possession of a corpse, each claiming to have loved Ophelia more. The First Quarto's stage direction — 'Hamlet leaps in after Laertes' — is disputed in later editions, and modern productions handle the moment variously. The textual uncertainty mirrors the scene's ethical uncertainty: the declaration of love ('forty thousand brothers / Could not, with all their quantity of love, / Make up my sum') is passionate but also bizarrely competitive. Hamlet loves Ophelia in retrospect, against Laertes, in public — a form of love her living self never got.
sources: Paul Werstine, 'The Textual Mystery of Hamlet' (1988)

trials.prompt: What do the gravediggers debate at the start of the scene?
options: Whether to dig Polonius's grave or Ophelia's first.
Whether Ophelia deserves Christian burial rites given her apparent suicide.
How much they should charge the King for digging.
Whether a new cemetery should be opened outside Elsinore.

trials.prompt: Whose skull does Hamlet particularly remember and mourn?
options: His father's.
Polonius's.
Yorick's, the court jester.
A lawyer unnamed.

trials.prompt: How does Hamlet's meditation on mortality in the graveyard differ from his 'To be, or not to be' soliloquy?
options: It is more abstract and philosophical than the soliloquy.
It is concrete and personal — particular skulls remembered as particular persons — rather than abstract.
It is delivered in Latin.
It concludes that death is to be welcomed without reservation.

trials.prompt: What does Hamlet's tracing of Alexander's dust through 'loam' and a 'beer-barrel' argue?
options: That great men should be embalmed rather than buried.
That even the noblest dust ends up stopping a bung-hole — a radical leveling of human greatness.
That beer is a fitter drink for a king than wine.
That Alexander was buried in Denmark.

trials.prompt: Why does Hamlet leap into Ophelia's grave after Laertes?
options: To rescue a sword he has dropped.
To insist — publicly and passionately — that he loved Ophelia more than Laertes could.
To prevent the priest from finishing the curtailed rites.
To attack Claudius, who is standing at the graveside.

trials.prompt: What does Hamlet declare about his love for Ophelia at the graveside?
options: That he never loved her and all his letters were pretense.
That forty thousand brothers could not have loved her as he did.
That he will follow her into death immediately.
That he forgives her for rejecting his suit.
